chat_messages.text: when was the symbol 'x' first started being used as 'unknown'?

content.text: when was the symbol 'x' first started being used as 'unknown'?

chat_messages.text:
The use of 'x' as a symbol for an unknown quantity is generally attributed to René Descartes in his work "La Géométrie" (1637), but the story is actually more complex and interesting.

The convention likely originated with medieval Spanish and Portuguese mathematicians, who used the Arabic word "šay" (شيء) meaning "thing" or "something" to represent the unknown quantity in algebraic problems. When translating Arabic mathematical texts to Spanish, they would use "xei" or "xay" as a transliteration.

Over time, this was abbreviated to just 'x', since it was a sound that existed in Spanish (represented by both 'x' and 'j'). When writing quickly in manuscripts, the 'x' was a convenient symbol that was rarely used for other mathematical purposes.

When Descartes published his work, he standardized this existing practice, using 'x', 'y', and 'z' for unknown quantities. His work was so influential that this notation became the standard across Europe and eventually worldwide.

It's worth noting that Arabic mathematicians like al-Khwarizmi (who essentially invented algebra) used words rather than symbols in their original works, and the evolution to symbolic notation was a gradual process that took place over several centuries.
